entries.wikiTitle: Cannabis in the United Arab Emirates
history: In 2022, the criminal penalties for drug-related crimes was reduced, with a possibility of no punishment for first-time offenders.
enforcementReality: Trafficking has a minimum five year sentence.
The United Arab Emirates prohibits the trafficking and sale of drugs, including cannabis, with drug use punishable of up to four years in prison.
In 2022, the criminal penalties for drug-related crimes was reduced, with a possibility of no punishment for first-time offenders.
notes: Cannabis in the United Arab Emirates is illegal.
The United Arab Emirates prohibits the trafficking and sale of drugs, including cannabis, with drug use punishable of up to four years in prison.
The UAE is not a significant cannabis producer or consumer, but is a major transshipment point for cannabis from Pakistan and Afghanistan, due to its free ports and heterogeneous population..

history: , 2010]], 2011]] eradicating cannabis in Kandahar in 2011]] Cannabis is illegal in Afghanistan.

It has been cultivated for centuries, and experienced relatively little interference until the 1970s, where after it became an issue both in international politics and in the finance of the series of wars which occurred in Afghanistan for forty years.
In 2010, the United Nations reported that Afghanistan was the world's top cannabis producer..
Baba Ku is a legendary folkloric character from Balkh in Afghanistan, a Sufi adherent who legendarily introduced hashish to Afghanistan..
Hashish had been made nominally illegal in 1957, allegedly mostly as a concession to US pressure, but persisted as a common drug in the country.
In 1972 Afghan authorities confiscated large amounts of refined heroin and hashish intended for export, revealing the increasingly international scope of drug production in the country.
US pressure on Kabul hashish syndicates in 1971 further increased the tension around the issue.
Royal prohibition.
In 1973, King Zahir Shah outlawed opium poppy and cannabis production, this time followed by genuine commitment to eradication, backed by $47 million in funding from the United States government.

Zahir Shah was deposed by his cousin Mohammed Daoud Khan that fall, who ended the monarchy and established himself as President, but the momentum of the hashish trade had been interrupted, and Western smugglers re-routed to Pakistani sources, so the 1973 harvest was minimal, as were harvests for several years following..
Kabul merchant Hyatullah Tohki transported hashish with his brother Amanullah, who worked at the American embassy in Kabul.

history: In 2012, Albania seized 21.2 metric tons of cannabis, nearly twice the amount seized in 2011.

Albania made global headlines and "placed itself on the cannabis map" in 2013 when the Albanian State Police (ASP) tried to shut down production in Lazarat, one of the heaviest producing towns in the mountainous southern regions that are said to be the "heartland" of Albanian cannabis production.
Cannabis is legal for medical and industrial purposes in Albania.
culture: The new industry took hold quickly despite attempts to curtail it, and it has been reported that in 1995 gunfights between farmers and police were common.

A 2009 report, however, noticed a reduction in the significance of Albanian cannabis exports, and that seizures of Albanian herbal cannabis had "become very rare in the last five years." culture: Following the pillars of the Malay Islamic Monarchy (MIB), Brunei has aligned the country's legislation and beliefs in accordance with its Malaysian culture, Islam, and the sultanate.
Both defendants were charged and pled guilty for the consumption of cannabis and methylamphetamine.
enforcementReality: Possession and use of cannabis and its resin is strictly prohibited in Brunei, both obtaining maximum sentences of 10 years and/or a USD$20,000 fine.
Possession of a pipe or any other equipment used to smoke cannabis is illegal and is punishable by a maximum sentence of 3 years in prison, and a USD$10,000 fine.
Withholding from providing a urine sample to law enforcement can be charged a fine of USD$5,000.

culture: Cannabis in Bolivia is illegal, but cultivated illicitly, mostly for domestic consumption.
enforcementReality: Bolivian law treats cannabis equally to cocaine, with possession of one gram punishable by 10–25 years in prison.

history: On February 27, 1929, the government passed the Dangerous Drugs Act which dealt with drug offences in the Bahamas, including cannabis.

In 1962, Act 26 amended the Act, providing a new definition of Indian hemp which, "includes all parts of the plant Cannabis Sativa whether growing or not from which the resin has not been extracted; the resin extracted from any part of such plant; and every compound, manufacture, salt derivative, mixture or preparation of such plant or resin".

Section 29(6) of the Dangerous Drugs Act deals with simple possession of cannabis::Where any drug to which this Act applies is, without the proper authority, found in the possession of any person or stored or kept in a place other than a place prescribed for the storage or keeping of such drug, such person, or the occupier or owner of such place or the owner of or other person responsible for the keeping of such d...

If a person is found with two or more packets containing cannabis, they will be presumed to be possessing that drug with the intent to supply it to others, unless they can prove the contrary under Section 22(3) of the Act.
culture: Cannabis in the Bahamas is illegal recreationally, but legal for medical and religious use..

history: Many "Happy" restaurants located in Phnom Penh, Siem Reap and Sihanoukville publicly offer food cooked with marijuana, or as a side garnish.
By 1961 in compliance with the Single Convention on Narcotics treaty it was technically made illegal but the law was unenforced, and marijuana was openly sold.
culture: Many "Happy" restaurants located in Phnom Penh, Siem Reap and Sihanoukville publicly offer food cooked with marijuana, or as a side garnish.
Cannabis has been traditionally grown in Cambodia and is a common ingredient in food.
As of 2009, the UNODC stated that cannabis production cultivation had "ceased to be a major concern" in Cambodia.
